options.text: Create a provider account share for the stored procedure and have the reporting app use that share.
options.explanation: Incorrect because a provider account share is for sharing data objects across accounts; it does not establish a controlled procedure call between two installed Native Apps in the same consumer account.

options.text: Have the reporting app call the provider account directly whenever it needs the ingestion procedure.
options.explanation: Incorrect because directly calling the provider account would bypass the installed app boundary and the consumer-approved inter-app connection workflow.

options.text: Copy the ingestion stored procedure into the reporting app package during every release.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because copying the ingestion procedure into the reporting app creates duplicated code, not an approved connection to the installed ingestion app and its current callable object.

[Snowflake Native App inter-app communication](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/inter-app-communication)

questions.stem:
A Snowflake Native App must let a consumer use a Cortex Agent to invoke tools defined by the app. The provider wants the tooling delivered and governed inside the installed app, without requiring a separate service tier outside Snowflake.

Which implementation aligns with Snowflake Native App support for agents and tools?

options.text: Package the agent or MCP server with the Native App using Snowflake's supported Native App agent and MCP server pattern.
options.explanation: Correct because snowflake documents that a Native App can create Cortex Agents, Snowflake-managed MCP servers, and SPCS-hosted MCP servers. These app-created objects can expose app-owned tools and services to Cortex Agents and Snowflake client infrastructure.

options.text: Host the MCP server only on the provider's public web service and call it from the installed app.
options.explanation: Incorrect because hosting the MCP server only on a provider public web service would not deliver the tooling as part of the installed Native App governed in the consumer account.

options.text: Use a Streamlit page as the only tool interface for the Cortex Agent.
options.explanation: Incorrect because streamlit can provide a user interface, but a Streamlit page alone does not register MCP tools or create a Cortex Agent tool surface.

options.text: Track agent usage with Marketplace analytics so the app can infer which tools are available.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because marketplace analytics observe listing activity; they do not create agents, MCP servers, or callable app-defined tools.

[Snowflake Native App Cortex Agents and MCP servers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/agents-mcp-servers)

questions.reference: [Snowflake Native App Cortex Agents and MCP servers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/agents-mcp-servers)

questions.stem:
A Streamlit page embedded in a Snowflake Native App cannot run a report because the selected consumer-owned table reference has not been approved yet. The request must be initiated from the app UI and completed by the consumer administrator in the consumer account.

What should the Streamlit page use?

options.text: Use the Native App Permission SDK to request and check the required consumer permission from the Streamlit UI.
options.explanation: Correct because snowflake provides the Python Permission SDK for Streamlit interfaces in Native Apps. The SDK lets the app check granted privileges and request references or privileged actions from the consumer through Snowsight.

options.text: Ask the provider to grant the table reference from the provider account after the UI request fails.
options.explanation: Incorrect because the provider account cannot grant access to a consumer-owned table reference; that approval must occur in the consumer account.

options.text: Edit `manifest.yml` in the installed app to add the missing table reference.
options.explanation: Incorrect because editing the provider source `manifest.yml` after installation does not grant an already installed app access to a consumer object. The app must request and receive consumer approval.

options.text: Grant the app owner's role directly to the Streamlit user who needs to run the report.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because exposing the app owner role to a Streamlit user is not the Native App permission model. Consumer approvals and application roles mediate app access.

[Snowflake Native App Permission SDK UI](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-ui)

questions.stem:
A connector Snowflake Native App has collected connection settings and selected data resources. Scheduled ingestion should not begin until the connector validates the final custom configuration and transitions out of its editable setup state.

Which Connector SDK flow should the app run next?

options.text: Run the Connector SDK finalize configuration flow after validation and before enabling ingestion.
options.explanation: Correct because the Connector SDK finalize configuration flow is the last wizard step after connection configuration. It validates the connector state and source configuration, can run final setup callbacks, and updates the connector to `STARTED` with configuration status `FINALIZED` when successful.

options.text: Create a compute pool to finalize the connector's selected connection settings and resources.
options.explanation: Incorrect because provisioning a compute pool does not validate Connector SDK configuration or move the connector from configurable setup state to finalized ingestion state.

options.text: Set a release directive for the application package so the connector leaves setup mode.
options.explanation: Incorrect because a release directive controls which application package version consumers receive; it does not finalize a specific consumer connector configuration.

options.text: Enable Marketplace auto-fulfillment to validate the connector settings and start ingestion.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because marketplace auto-fulfillment controls listing access and fulfillment. It does not validate connector settings or enable ingestion for an installed connector.

[Snowflake Native SDK for Connectors finalize configuration](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/connector-sdk/flow/finalize_configuration)

questions.stem:
A consumer reports invalid upstream credentials for a connector Snowflake Native App. They want scheduled ingestion to stop while the team investigates, but they need to keep the connection settings and resource definitions available for troubleshooting.

Which action should the app support?

options.text: Use the Connector SDK pause connector flow so ingestion stops without removing connector configuration.
options.explanation: Correct because the Connector SDK pause connector flow is available after finalize configuration and is designed to manipulate connector status after launch. It calls `PUBLIC.PAUSE_CONNECTOR()`, can pause connector-specific tasks, and updates connector status to `PAUSED` while configuration remains finalized.

options.text: Drop the installed application so all connector activity is stopped immediately.
options.explanation: Incorrect because dropping the installed application removes the app installation rather than preserving connector state for diagnosis.

options.text: Delete every configured resource to force the connector back into setup mode.
options.explanation: Incorrect because deleting configured resources removes the metadata the team needs to inspect and repair the connector setup.

options.text: Suspend a warehouse and leave the connector active until the credentials are repaired.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because suspending a warehouse only affects compute availability. It does not express the Connector SDK pause state or run the connector lifecycle hooks that pause ingestion cleanly.

[Snowflake Native SDK for Connectors pause connector](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/connector-sdk/flow/pause_connector)

questions.stem:
A provider's Snowflake Native App setup script creates a Streamlit object named `APP_SCHEMA.REVIEW_UI` from packaged UI files. After installation, consumer users assigned to the app's `APP_PUBLIC` application role must be able to open that UI.

Which privilege grants should the setup script include?

options.text: Grant `USAGE` on `APP_SCHEMA` and `USAGE` on `STREAMLIT APP_SCHEMA.REVIEW_UI` to the `APP_PUBLIC` application role.
options.explanation: Correct because snowflake Native App Streamlit setup examples grant `USAGE` on the schema and `USAGE` on the Streamlit object to an application role after `CREATE STREAMLIT`; creating the object alone does not expose it to app users.

options.text: Grant `SELECT` on the Streamlit object to `APP_PUBLIC` after `CREATE STREAMLIT` completes.
options.explanation: Incorrect because `SELECT` is a data access privilege for objects such as tables and views. A Streamlit object is opened through `USAGE` on the Streamlit object and its schema.

options.text: Add the Streamlit file path only to `manifest.yml` and rely on artifact metadata for access.
options.explanation: Incorrect because the manifest can identify packaged Streamlit files or a default Streamlit app, but artifact metadata does not grant an application role access to the created object.

options.text: Grant `OWNERSHIP` on the application package to the consumer roles that should open the UI.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because consumer users receive privileges through application roles in the installed app. They do not need, and should not receive, `OWNERSHIP` on the provider's application package.

[Snowflake Native App Streamlit setup](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/adding-streamlit)

questions.reference: [Grant privileges on Streamlit in a Native App](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/adding-streamlit#grant-privileges-on-a-streamlit-object)

questions.stem:
A Snowflake Native App can optionally create a warehouse in the consumer account after the consumer approves the request. The consumer's security reviewer must see why the app is requesting `CREATE WAREHOUSE` before approving installation in Snowsight.

Where should the provider declare this request?

options.text: Declare `CREATE WAREHOUSE` in the manifest `privileges` block with a description explaining why the app requests it.
options.explanation: Correct because automated privilege grants are declared in the app manifest. Snowflake's example places `CREATE WAREHOUSE` under `privileges` with a `description`, which is shown to consumers during the install approval flow.

options.text: Create an application role named `CREATE_WAREHOUSE` so the consumer can grant it during installation.
options.explanation: Incorrect because an application role controls access to objects exposed by the app. Naming an application role `CREATE_WAREHOUSE` would not request or grant an account-level consumer privilege.

options.text: Run `GRANT CREATE WAREHOUSE` in `setup.sql` before creating the optional warehouse.
options.explanation: Incorrect because `setup.sql` cannot unilaterally grant account privileges in the consumer account. The app can use the privilege only after the consumer approves a manifest-declared request.

options.text: Define a table reference callback named `CREATE WAREHOUSE` for the consumer approval flow.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because references are for consumer objects such as tables or warehouses that the consumer binds to the app. `CREATE WAREHOUSE` is an account privilege request, not a table reference callback.

[Snowflake Native App automated privilege grants](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-auto-privs)

questions.stem:
A provider has added version `V2` patch `3` to a Snowflake Native App application package. Before publishing it to consumers, the provider wants a development account to install exactly that package build and validate the packaged setup script and artifacts. The test must not change the package release directive.

Which action should the provider take?

options.text: Create a test application from the application package using the specific `VERSION V2` and `PATCH 3`.
options.explanation: Correct because snowflake allows creating an application from a defined application package version or patch. Specifying `VERSION V2 PATCH 3` tests that exact build without changing what consumers receive through a release directive.

options.text: Set the default release directive to `V2` patch `3` before running the development test.
options.explanation: Incorrect because setting the default release directive changes the version selected for consumers instead of only installing the build in a development account for regression testing.

options.text: Submit a Marketplace listing revision so reviewers install exactly `V2` patch `3`.
options.explanation: Incorrect because a Marketplace listing revision is part of distribution and review. It is not the local mechanism for installing an exact version and patch from an application package.

options.text: Execute `setup.sql` directly in the provider database to validate the packaged artifacts.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because executing `setup.sql` directly in the provider database bypasses application package installation behavior and does not validate how packaged artifacts install as an app.

[Install and test a Snowflake Native App locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.reference: [Install and test a Snowflake Native App locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.stem:
A provider includes a `version` block in `manifest.yml`, but also defines versions and patches for the application package with Snowflake package SQL commands. The two sources do not match.

Which version metadata does Snowflake use for the application package?

options.text: Snowflake uses the version and patch definitions from `CREATE APPLICATION PACKAGE` or `ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE` because they take precedence over manifest version metadata.
options.explanation: Correct because the Snowflake manifest reference states that versions and patches defined with `CREATE APPLICATION PACKAGE` or `ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE` take precedence over version information defined in the manifest file.

options.text: Use `artifacts.readme` as the authoritative source for version labels when the manifest and SQL differ.
options.explanation: Incorrect because `artifacts.readme` points to consumer-facing documentation. It does not define authoritative application package version or patch metadata.

options.text: Use `setup.sql` to overwrite the application package version during installation.
options.explanation: Incorrect because `setup.sql` creates objects during app installation. It does not decide precedence between manifest version metadata and package-level version definitions.

options.text: Use the Marketplace listing title as the version name for package version resolution.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because a Marketplace listing title is presentation metadata for the listing. It does not define package versions or patches used by Snowflake.

[Snowflake Native App manifest reference](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/manifest-reference)

questions.stem:
A provider's Snowflake Native App must expose a secure view over a table stored in a separate provider database outside the application package. Consumers need governed read access through the installed app, but the provider does not want to grant consumers direct access to the outside database table.

Which packaging pattern supports this requirement?

options.text: Grant `REFERENCE_USAGE` on the outside database to the application package, create a view inside the package that references the table, and grant access to that view through an application role.
options.explanation: Correct because snowflake documents that provider objects outside the application package require `REFERENCE_USAGE` on the external database to the application package. The provider then creates a view inside the package that references the outside table and exposes the app-facing view through application-role grants.

options.text: Grant `SELECT` on the outside table directly to the consumer account for the installed app.
options.explanation: Incorrect because direct `SELECT` grants to the consumer account bypass the Native App's package objects and application-role permission model, which is the governed access path in this scenario.

options.text: Add the outside database name to manifest artifacts so it is packaged with the app.
options.explanation: Incorrect because manifest artifacts describe packaged files and resources. They do not grant the application package privilege to reference a provider database outside the package.

options.text: Request a consumer table reference for the provider-owned table outside the application package.
options.explanation:
Incorrect because consumer references are for consumer-side objects that the consumer binds to the app. They are not used to access provider-owned databases outside the application package.

[Snowflake Native App shared data content](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/preparing-data-content)
